Harvard University Press, 1981.</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><aut authindexid="Barlow, Jack M." listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>Jack</nfirst> <nmid>M.</nmid> <nlast>Barlow</nlast> <ndeg dgr="PHD"/></aut>
<autaff><instit>University of Tennessee</instit></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: In A Different Voice: Carol Gilligan. Harvard University Press, 1981.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

In A Different Voice

Carol Gilligan. Harvard University Press, 1981.

Jack Barlow PHD

Jack Barlow

Jack Barlow  

Taking on the giants of psychological developmental theory (the likes of Erikson, Kohlberg, Freud, Piaget, Vaillant, Levinson) smacks of grandiosity. Doing so with grace, perspective, and intelligence transforms the shades of grandiosity into a demonstration that sensible and intelligent research still is being done in the real world. Maybe it is “Harvard” or her association with David McClelland or just the confluence of these factors with native ingenuity, but Carol Gilligan's In A Different Voice ought to inspire a retrofit of graduate psychology curriculum for the next decade.

Her hypothesis is simple. The current theories of psychological development are biased by a single track comprehension of maturity—separation-individuation. This is a motif for all modern theories. There is nothing wrong here, except, the separation-individuation track has a distinctly male turn to it. In other words men develop in that way but, typically, women hardly identify with it.

Gilligan studies, among other things, the relation between identity and moral choice as they are integrated during the long transition from adolescence to adulthood. For the male “… individual achievement rivets the male imagination.

Requests for reprints should be sent to Jack M. Barlow, Ph.D., Associate Professor, Department of Psychology, University of Tennessee, Knoxville, Tennessee 37916.

85

responseSet.title: Prestage Versus Defensive Splitting and the Borderline Personality: A Rorschach Analysis
responseSet.authorMast: Steven H. Cooper and  David Arnow
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Prestage Versus Defensive Splitting and the Borderline Personality

A Rorschach Analysis

Steven Cooper PHD and David Arnow PHD

David Arnow

David Arnow  

This paper reviews theoretical contributions by Kernberg (1975) and by Stolorow and Lachmann (1980) regarding defensive modes of splitting and suggests possible Rorschach manifestations for a variety of levels of defensive organization among borderline patients. Defensive operations vary among patients with different levels of severity of borderline psychopathology. Specifically, it is proposed that some borderline patients rely on splitting as an active attempt to mitigate the experience of intense ambivalence conflicts toward others, as described by Kernberg; another group of borderline patients is seen as having pathology that revolves around a weakly integrated representational world, poor self-object differentiation, and prestage developmental levels of defense, as described by Stolorow and Lachmann. The Rorschach is a valuable diagnostic tool in distinguishing between psychopathology involving defenses against structural conflict and psychopathology based on an arrest in development at the level of prestage defensive operations.

New York: Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 1984</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><aut authindexid="Lewis, Helen B." listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>Helen</nfirst> <nmid>Block</nmid> <nlast>Lewis</nlast> <ndeg dgr="PHD"/></aut>
<autaff><instit>Yale University</instit></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Assault on Truth: Freud's Suppression of the Seduction Theory: Jeffrey Moussaieff Masson. New York: Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 1984
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Assault on Truth: Freud's Suppression of the Seduction Theory

Jeffrey Moussaieff Masson. New York: Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 1984

Helen Lewis PHD

Helen Lewis

Helen Lewis  

This book deserves our admiration for its masterly review of historical events in the development of psychoanalysis. It should be read by psychoanalysts not only for its enormous fund of skillfully assembled information about the formative years of Freud's thinking, but for its story of how new information was treated by some leaders of the psychoanalytic establishment. In the guise of protecting psychoanalysis, this information was dismissed as harmful. It is precisely such a well-meaning upholding of psychoanalytic doctrine that can throttle its growth. This is not to say that I agree with Masson's interpretations of Freud's behavior about the seduction theory, nor do I agree with Masson's estimate of the ultimate significance of the events he details. Although some of Masson's interpretations are made in the best Freudian style, this reader remains unconvinced that, in what Masson calls a “failure of courage,” Freud suppressed the truth. Nor did Freud's abandonment of the seduction theory lead to the present-day “sterility” of psychoanalysis, as Masson believes. Rather, the spurious need to defend psychoanalysis that Masson encountered during his investigations has also made many institutes sterile places. Masson thus confounds the limitations of some parts of the psychoanalytic establishment with the future of psychoanalysis itself.

Requests for reprints should be sent to Helen Block Lewis, Ph.D., Professor Emerita, Department of Psychology, Yale University, Box 11A Yale Station, New Haven, CT 06520.

<autaff><instit>Storm Clinics Evanston, III</instit></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Feeling Bad and Feeling Badly
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Feeling Bad and Feeling Badly

Kenneth Isaacs PHD

Kenneth Isaacs

Kenneth Isaacs  

An alternative psychoanalytic theory of affect is presented. It is suggested here that affective response consists of rapidly repeated generation of a series of brief responses each enduring for fractions of seconds, continuing over whatever period conscious or unconscious focus on a specific mental content is maintained. The subjective impression, however, is that of a continuous steady state. The experiencing of affect is, in this way, similar to that of sensory process experience in general. Affect cannot store. Since affect does not store, there is no damming of affect and no requirement for affect expression or discharge. A sequence of theoretical ramifications and elaborations results in a redefinition of the classic neuroses and some other disorders. Extensive paths of implications of affect actualities and affect dynamics are suggested as impinging on various aspects of human psychology.

<autaff><addr><ln>Faculté de Médecine</ln><ln>Paris</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: 200 Years of Psychotherapy: The Common Curative Elements in Suggestion and Affect
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

200 Years of Psychotherapy

The Common Curative Elements in Suggestion and Affect

Leon Chertok MD

Leon Chertok

Faculté de Médecine
Paris

Leon Chertok  

The therapeutic relationship entered the field of experimental science towards the end of the 18th century, with Mesmer's animal magnetism.

It subsequently recurs under a variety of names. In particular it appears in the form of suggestion, whose operational force is essentially affective.

Freud was wary of suggestion and thought that it could be dealt with by cognitive means (interpretation, insight, and so on). But there is a growing realization, today, that the bond implied in suggestion develops at an archaic, preverbal, psycho-biological level, irreducible in terms of the intellectual methods employed to date. Hence the elusive element encountered in all psychotherapies.

It is my feeling that investigators would do well to overcome their ancient and still persistent prejudices and seek to elucidate this mysterious, though fundamental, concept.

responseSet.title: Brief Dynamic Psychotherapy in Community Mental Health Settings: Providing a Holding Environment on a Short-Term Basis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Brief Dynamic Psychotherapy in Community Mental Health Settings

Providing a Holding Environment on a Short-Term Basis

John Rosegrant PHD

John Rosegrant

John Rosegrant  

Because of funding limitations, Community Mental Health therapists must often apply brief therapy techniques to an inappropriate clientele. Previously developed techniques hasten treatment by means of the therapist actively focusing interpretations on either preoedipal or oedipal issues. An actively focused technique would be inappropriate for most Community Mental Health patients, however, because it would not effectively address their strong dependency needs. Active focusing would not provide the holding environment which is crucial in treating dependent patients. More appropriate is a moderate amount of focusing in the context of tolerant, unfocused listening to provide a holding environment. This approach is illustrated with case examples.

responseSet.title: A Type of Identification with Christ
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Type of Identification with Christ

Milton Eber PHD and Guillermo Marcovici MD

Guillermo Marcovici

Guillermo Marcovici  

A patient with grandiose and exhibitionistic characteristics, who relentlessly pursued women in an unending series of conquests, is presented to demonstrate a particular aspect of a Christ identification which, to our knowledge, has not been previously discussed in the psychoanalytic literature. Specifically, he unconsciously identified with the adult Christ image in which physical perfection is emphasized—an image that evokes adoration and has the power to excite the senses.

<artinfo arttype="ART" lang="en" j="PPSY" id="PPSY.001.0301A" ISSN="0736-9735"><artyear>1984</artyear><artvol actual="1">1</artvol><artiss>4</artiss><artpgrg style="arabic">301-318</artpgrg><arttitle lang="en">Diagnostic Issues in Borderline Disorders</arttitle><artauth hidden="false"><aut authindexid="Raifman, Irving" listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>Irving</nfirst> <nlast>Raifman</nlast> <ndeg dgr="PHD"/></aut>
<autaff><addr><ln>Chevy Chase, Maryland</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Diagnostic Issues in Borderline Disorders
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Diagnostic Issues in Borderline Disorders

Irving Raifman PHD

Irving Raifman

Chevy Chase, Maryland

Irving Raifman  

Current theories and psychological test findings on borderline disorders reveal that there are patients whose clinical behaviors are similar to one another; they are afflicted with such symptoms of emotional instability as intense problematic interpersonal relationships, and a tendency to injure themselves or to set up situations in which they are victimized. At best, these patients are difficult to treat. They manifest traits representative of various personality disturbances (see, e.g., American Psychiatric Association Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, 3rd ed.), as well as severe affective disorders. At times, they appear schizophrenic; often, their behavior is neurotic. As they are in transition, they cannot be diagnosed at a given moment in time, except by inference from a longitudinal study of their behavior, and by an analysis of the transference and countertransference that is being experienced.

In this author's opinion, many psychological test findings lack reliability and validity and there is no basis for the label borderline disorder as a stable, discrete entity, at least not yet.

<autaff><instit>Michigan State University</instit></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: A Type of Transference Based on Identification with an Abusing Parent
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Type of Transference Based on Identification with an Abusing Parent

Bertram Karon PHD

Bertram Karon

Bertram Karon  

A type of transference is described in which the patient treats the therapist not as if the latter were his father or mother, but as if the therapist were the patient and the patient reenacts the role of mother and father. This usually occurs when the therapy session seems irritating, exhausting or anxiety-provoking; this is particularly frequent with borderline and schizophrenic patients. An awareness of the significance of this type of transference will allow the therapist to make an unpleasant therapeutic situation into a useful therapeutic intervention.

<autaff><instit>University of Missouri Kansas City, School of Medicine</instit></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Action Language and Clinical Technique: A Reply to Spence
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Action Language and Clinical Technique

A Reply to Spence

Stephen Appelbaum PHD

Stephen Appelbaum

Stephen Appelbaum  

In this paper I reply to Spence's criticism of Schafer's action language theory as applied to work with patients. Spence assumes that such an application will replace the analytic situation with adversarial confrontation. This would supposedly come about largely through badgering the patient to produce reasons for his behavior and blaming him both for the behavior and for not producing the right reasons for it. He also foresees an overly specific and desiccated language replacing the metaphors and allusions that accurately portray experience. Spence's concerns rest on his assuming that Schafer's ideas would be applied clinically in ways that no one need do even if employing the ideas theoretically.

Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1982.</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><aut authindexid="Wolstein, Benjamin" listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>Benjamin</nfirst> <nlast>Wolstein</nlast> <ndeg dgr="PHD"/></aut>
<autaff><instit>W.A. White Institute of Psychiatry</instit><addr><ln>Psychoanalysis and Psychology</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Psychiatrist of America: The Life of Harry Stack Sullivan: H. S. Perry. Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1982.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychiatrist of America: The Life of Harry Stack Sullivan

H. S. Perry. Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1982.

Benjamin Wolstein PHD

Benjamin Wolstein

Psychoanalysis and Psychology

Benjamin Wolstein  

Sullivan's characterization of the self is, by now, well-known. He maintains that the self is made up of reflected appraisals. The self, in other words, suggests a mosaic comprised of interpersonal responses mirroring the interpersonal responses of significant others in the social and cultural surround. Such is his statement of the issue, operationist through and through. Whatever else there may be to the overall psychology of the self it extends beyond the interpersonal perspective. Only the reflected self's interpersonal relations are open and amenable to the operationist inquiry.

But, strictly adopted, the clinical effect of such interpersonal delimitation is quite serious, and it has continuing impact on the field of therapeutic inquiry to which it is applied. The reason for this is already implied: interpersonal psychoanalysts, strongly committed to the study of the interpersonal dimension of the psyche, do not look to explore its other dimensions. This strong clinical emphasis on the operational interface of personality inevitably demarcates the working range of interpersonal therapeutic inquiry.

This paper was presented in part, with minor changes, with the title “The Interpersonal Perspective of the American School,” at the Weekend Meeting of the W.A. White Psychoanalytic Society, April 1983, Walker Valley, New York.

Requests for reprints should be sent to Benjamin Wolstein, Ph.D., 2 West 67 Street, New York, NY 10023.

165

<artinfo arttype="ART" lang="en" j="PPSY" id="PPSY.001.0061A" ISSN="0736-9735"><artyear>1984</artyear><artvol actual="1">1</artvol><artiss>1</artiss><artpgrg style="arabic">61-76</artpgrg><arttitle lang="en">Hypnoanalysis</arttitle><artsub lang="en">With Particular Emphasis on the Borderline Patient<ftnx r="F001">1</ftnx></artsub><artauth hidden="false"><aut authindexid="Fromm, Erika" listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>Erika</nfirst> <nlast>Fromm</nlast> <ndeg dgr="MD"/></aut>
<autaff><instit>University of Chicago</instit></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Hypnoanalysis: With Particular Emphasis on the Borderline Patient1
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Hypnoanalysis

With Particular Emphasis on the Borderline Patient1

Erika Fromm MD

Erika Fromm

Erika Fromm  

In the first part of this paper a short overview of the history of hypnotherapy and hypnoanalysis is given. Within the modality of hypnosis, the patient can make contact with his unconscious much faster than in the waking state. Primary process thinking and vivid imagery play a great role. Hypnotic imagery and the hypnotic state per se are “royal roads to the unconscious” on which the hypnoanalyst must attempt to walk with care but also with confidence, so that defenses are not pierced prematurely and the patient can learn to use to the fullest extent his innate healthy coping potential. The second part of the paper deals with the newest development in the field of hypnoanalysis, the treatment of the borderline patient with specialized hypnotic imagery techniques that have been found to help such patients overcome splitting, achieve object constancy, and good internalized object and self representations.

<autaff><instit>University of Colorado School of Medicine</instit></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Research in Dynamic Psychotherapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Research in Dynamic Psychotherapy

Herbert Schlesinger PHD

Herbert Schlesinger

Herbert Schlesinger  

The National Institute of Mental Health has been concerned about the imbalance between the orientation of psychotherapy largely practiced in this country, largely psychodynamic and its psychotherapy research portfolio, largely behavioral. This imbalance clearly reflects the greater ease in operationalizing behavioral concepts and the relative simplicity and brevity of behavioral treatments that make it easier to design and execute research projects. The special requirements of a relationship—dependent therapy such as dynamic psychotherapy or psychoanalysis are also inconsistent with the simple clinical trial research paradigm, loosely drawn from psychopharmacology, which is far more suitable for behavior therapy than for dynamic psychotherapy.

The Psychosocial Treatments Branch of NIMH held two conferences during 1982 and 1983 at which groups of experts in dynamic psychotherapy and research examined the feasibility of mounting a special research program on dynamic psychotherapy. The consensus was that such a research program would be possible but that methods would need to be developed to describe and measure the key variables in dynamic psychotherapy including transference, therapeutic alliance and resistance and to develop outcome measures related to interpersonal functioning. Patients for such research could not be defined by DSM III criteria alone but should have interpersonal or psychosomatic difficulties that particularly require this form of treatment. The work of skilled therapists, not beginners, should be studied.

A further recommendation by the panel of experts was that the research be focused on long-term as well as short-term and time-limited psycho-therapies and that programmatic support be offered for the development of research centers in dynamic psychotherapy. In such centers, the needed critical mass of researchers, therapists, and patients could be concentrated. In them new generations of psychotherapy researchers could also be trained.

<autaff><instit>Postgraduate Center for Mental Health</instit></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Treatment of Narcissism
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Treatment of Narcissism

Joan Freyberg PHD

Joan Freyberg

Joan Freyberg  

This paper discusses the need for change in emphasis from metapsychological debate to a sharp clinical focus on the complex nature of narcissistic pathology. Narcissism is viewed as a dimension of psychopathology found at all levels of psychic functioning, at the core of which are characteristic ego and superego deficits around self-cohesion, self-continuity, and self-esteem regulation. It is argued that, if the definition of conflict is not viewed too narrowly, traditional Freudian or ego-psychological techniques are applicable and that the treatment of narcissism does not require a new theory, separate from that of object relations. Clinical material is presented to illustrate that all psychological phenomena are over-determined and contain aspects of unresolved preoedipal and oedipal conflicts. There are critical selfhood aspects at each stage of development which must be understood and interpreted, in addition to the traditional structural conflicts. It is stressed that highly developed skills in listening and in interpreting are required in order to discern the narcissistic and object-relations aspects of the clinical material and that the countertransference around the analyst's own narcissism needs particular attention.

responseSet.title: Psychodynamic Research Perspectives on Development, Psychopathology, and Treatment in Later Life
responseSet.authorMast: Nancy E. Miller and  Bertram J. Cohler
responseSet.authorCitation: Miller, N. E. & Cohler, B. J.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychodynamic Research Perspectives on Development, Psychopathology, and Treatment in Later Life

Nancy Miller PHD and Bertram Cohler PHD

Bertram Cohler

Bertram Cohler  

Over 700 psychoanalysts and other mental health professionals and investigators recently gathered at the National Institute of Mental Health in Washington, to consider research on the interplay of personality, biology, and society in shaping adaptation to aging. The convening of this meeting, co-sponsored by the Center for Studies of the Mental Health of the Aging and the Psychosocial Treatments Research Branch, marked the first time that NIMH had sponsored a conference in which the clinical and theoretical contributions of psychoanalysis to the study of normal and pathological development had been so explicitly recognized.

Many distinguished psychologists-psychoanalysts served on the formal conference program, among others, including Drs. Diana Hartley, Herbert Schlesinger, and Hans Strupp.

Requests for reprints should be sent to Dr. Nancy E. Miller, Chief, Clinical Research Program, Center for Studies of the Mental Health of the Aging, National Institute of Mental Health, 5600 Fishers Lane, Room 11-C-03, Rockville, Maryland, 20857.

77

responseSet.title: Merger Fantasies in Individual Inpatient Therapy with Schizophrenic Patients
responseSet.authorMast: Nanette C.  Auerhahn and  Michael B.  Moskowitz
responseSet.authorCitation: Auerhahn,, N. C. & Moskowitz,, M. B.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Merger Fantasies in Individual Inpatient Therapy with Schizophrenic Patients**

Nanette Auerhahn, PHD and Michael Moskowitz, PHD

Michael Moskowitz,

Michael Moskowitz,  

This paper examines some of the differences between individual outpatient and individual inpatient psychoanalytic psychotherapy to illustrate that individual therapy on an inpatient unit is a qualitatively different modality from its outpatient counterpart with consequences for work with schizophrenics. The paper explores the manner in which the unique parameters of inpatient work interact with, sustain, and exploit the pathology of schizophrenic patients. Its focus is, specifically, on the handling of the boundary disturbances which characterize schizophrenic psychoses, and the manner in which the fluidity of boundaries is reinforced and the tendency toward symbiotic relatedness enhanced on an inpatient unit. As a result, a central fantasy develops in many schizophrenic inpatients, one wherein the therapist is transformed into the omniscient, omnipotent early mother. This fantasy, which can be considered an institutional transference, helps explain the frequently observed phenomenon that many patients show substantial symptomatic improvement while in the hospital, only to have their symptoms return after discharge.

responseSet.title: The Sexually Abused Patient and the Abusing Therapist: A Study in Sadomasochistic Relationships
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Sexually Abused Patient and the Abusing Therapist

A Study in Sadomasochistic Relationships

Sydney Smith PHD

Sydney Smith

Sydney Smith  

Coming to light with increasing frequency are cases in which psychoanalysts and psychotherapists engage in sexually abusing behavior toward their patients. Some observers of this sordid situation have suggested that the very nature of the therapeutic or analytic relationship, with its counterplay of nonverbal transference attachments, makes the therapist susceptible to falling into the role of seducer. This paper describes cases in which the sexual contact becomes injurious and traumatic for the patient. It explores the psychological dynamics of such cases, looks at the possible conscious and unconscious motives of the therapist, and relates those issues to the pathological background of the patient.

responseSet.title: Orality and Field-Dependence
responseSet.authorMast: Richard M. O'Neill,  Roger P. Greenberg and  Seymour Fisher
responseSet.authorCitation: O'Neill, R. M., Greenberg, R. P. & Fisher, S.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Orality and Field-Dependence

Richard O'Neill PHD , Roger Greenberg PHD and Seymour Fisher PHD

Seymour Fisher

Seymour Fisher  

Freud's oral character and Witkin's field-dependent person are theoretically similar and empirically have been shown to display many of the same traits and behaviors. This investigation examined their relationship in a group of normal adult women. Three of six oral variables were significantly related to Witkin's Rod-and-frame error score of field-dependence. Field-dependent women had more spontaneous oral thoughts, more negative attitudes toward food and eating, and recalled fewer food than nonfood words when tested in a learning task. These results suggest that field-dependent women are orally conflicted, and that the concept of orality may be a link between the externality exhibited by the obese, by the alcoholic, and by the field-dependent person.

<autaff affid="af001"><instit>Yeshiva University, Yale University and the William Alanson White Institute</instit></autaff><autaff affid="af002"><instit>California School of Professional Psychology, San Diego Psychoanalytic Institute and the University of California, San Diego</instit></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Dimensions of Internalized Object Relations in Borderline and Schizophrenic Patients
responseSet.authorMast: Walter E. Spear and  Alan Sugarman
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dimensions of Internalized Object Relations in Borderline and Schizophrenic Patients

Walter Spear PHD and Alan Sugarman PHD

Alan Sugarman

Alan Sugarman  

This paper explores two dimensions of internalized object relations in borderline and schizophrenic patients using Rorschach scales. A thematic and a structural Rorschach measure of object relations is applied to the Rorschachs of two borderline groups (an infantile personality group and an obsessive-paranoid group) and a schizophrenic group. The findings suggest the value of both a multidimensional definition of internalized object relations and the use of such an approach to clarifying the diagnostic concept of borderline personality disorder.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysts' and Psychotherapists' Conflicts About Setting Fees
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysts' and Psychotherapists' Conflicts About Setting Fees

Ella Lasky PHD

Ella Lasky

New York

Ella Lasky  

This paper explores the intrapsychic conflicts experienced by 60 psychoanalysts and psychotherapists concerning fee-setting. Two thirds of the sample surveyed were particularly affected by this dilemma. The conflict is traced to (1) society's conflicting messages about money, (2) the lack of professional training concerning money matters, and (3) the enormous social mobility of many practitioners. Although both males and females were found to harbor ambivalent feelings regarding fee-setting, the women were more acutely aware of their conflict and therefore more pained by it. Finally, practitioners, depending on their individual character types, have different attitudes concerning fee-setting. Seven common problems are illustrated.
